description: The whitetip reef shark is a slender, medium-sized shark found throughout the Indo-Pacific, easily identified by the distinctive white tips on its dorsal and caudal fins. Unlike most sharks, it can lie motionless on the seafloor and pump water over its gills. It is primarily nocturnal, spending the day resting in caves and under ledges before hunting in groups at night.
funFact: Whitetip reef sharks hunt in coordinated packs at night, chasing reef fish into crevices and coral formations where multiple sharks simultaneously wedge into gaps to extract hidden prey — a rare form of cooperative hunting behavior for sharks.
habitat: Coral reef environments throughout the Indo-Pacific, from East Africa and the Red Sea to the Pacific coast of Central America. Typically found at depths of 8–40 m, closely associated with the reef substrate in clear lagoon and seaward reef habitats.
geographicRangeDescription: Found across the tropical Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea and East Africa through the Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia, and Oceania to the Pacific coast of Central America. Range includes the Maldives, Seychelles, northern Australia, the Great Barrier Reef, Micronesia, and the Galápagos Islands. One of the most reef-faithful sharks — individuals may spend years within a small home range on a single reef.
diet: Opportunistic nocturnal predator feeding on reef fish (wrasses, parrotfish, surgeonfish), octopus, squid, crustaceans, and lobster. Hunts in coordinated groups after dark, flushing prey from crevices and coral heads.
size: Typically 1.4–1.7 m (4.6–5.6 ft); maximum recorded 2.1 m (6.9 ft). Females grow slightly larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: No reliable global estimate — declining across Indo-Pacific range

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Reef fish, octopus, squid, crustaceans, lobster

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Slow — low litter sizes and late maturity limit recovery

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Can remain stationary on the seafloor by actively pumping water over gills; exceptional low-light vision for nocturnal reef hunting

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Indo-Pacific from East Africa and the Red Sea to the Pacific coast of Central America

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: 1–40 m (3–130 ft), primarily 8–30 m on reef slopes

threats.name: Targeted Fishing
threats.description: Actively hunted across much of Southeast Asia and the Indian Ocean for their fins and meat, both for local consumption and the international fin trade.

threats.name: Coral Reef Degradation
threats.description: Entirely dependent on healthy reef ecosystems for shelter and hunting. Coral bleaching, Crown-of-Thorns starfish outbreaks, and coastal development have reduced reef quality across their range.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Caught incidentally in reef gillnets, artisanal longlines, and fish traps targeting other reef species throughout the Indo-Pacific.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Warming seas drive repeated coral bleaching events, reducing shelter availability and disrupting prey communities that the species depends on.

actionItems.title: Support Coral Reef Conservation
actionItems.description: Organizations like the Coral Triangle Initiative and Reef Check work to protect the reef ecosystems that whitetip reef sharks depend on. Donations and advocacy matter.

actionItems.title: Choose Reef-Safe Dive Tourism
actionItems.description: Support dive operators that follow no-touch guidelines and advocate for reef MPAs. Responsible dive tourism creates economic incentives for reef protection over extractive fishing.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Seafood
actionItems.description: Use the Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch guide to avoid reef fish from unsustainable fisheries in the Indo-Pacific that generate bycatch of reef sharks.

actionItems.title: Reduce Your Carbon Footprint
actionItems.description: Coral bleaching driven by ocean warming is one of the biggest threats to reef shark habitat. Supporting climate action protects the reef ecosystems these sharks call home.

healthMetrics.label: IUCN Status
healthMetrics.value: Vulnerable — uplisted from Near Threatened in 2015; declining trend noted across assessments

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — documented declines across most of the Indo-Pacific, especially in Southeast Asia

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Declining — coral reef degradation from bleaching, coastal development, and destructive fishing

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — reef fish heavily overfished across Southeast Asia and Indian Ocean

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — targeted for fins and meat across Southeast Asia; bycatch in reef fisheries widespread

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Limited — CITES Appendix II since 2016; few MPAs adequately enforced in the Indo-Pacific

populationTrendMeta.note: No reliable global population estimate exists. Numbers shown are rough models based on documented regional declines in the Indo-Pacific, particularly in Southeast Asia where reef fish extraction and fin trade are intensive. Actual global totals are unknown.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Coral Triangle
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef fish heavily overfished across Indonesia, Philippines, and Malaysia; destructive practices further reduce prey availability

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indian Ocean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Widespread reef overfishing and blast fishing reduce prey communities across East Africa and South Asian reefs

preyDeclineRegions.name: Pacific Islands
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef fish moderately depleted; some islands recovering under established MPAs

preyDeclineRegions.name: Red Sea
preyDeclineRegions.note: Localized reef degradation and targeted fishing pressure on reef fish communities

fishingPressureRegions.name: Coral Triangle
fishingPressureRegions.note: Heavily targeted for fins and meat; destructive fishing methods (blast, cyanide) common on reefs across the region

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indian Ocean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted in reef fisheries throughout East Africa and South Asia; minimal enforcement in most areas

fishingPressureRegions.name: Pacific Islands
fishingPressureRegions.note: Bycatch in reef gillnets and longlines; some island nations have established shark sanctuaries

fishingPressureRegions.name: Red Sea
fishingPressureRegions.note: Fin trade and food fisheries active; enforcement patchy along the broader coastline
